sources.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 (Linear Momentum and Collisions) — Test Prep for AP® Courses
sources.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; the live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per the PRD, mirroring the OpenStax-sourced items already in questions.json)

meta.notes: Physics (CPBS) momentum/collision items. Question stems and the four answer options were scraped verbatim from the OpenStax College Physics for AP Courses 2e 'Test Prep for AP Courses' multiple-choice items (single-best-answer, four options each). OpenStax publishes the answer key only to instructors, so each correctAnswer was independently verified from the same CC BY-NC-SA source text (Newton's third law, conservation of momentum, equal-mass elastic exchange) or by direct calculation; answerProvenance = verified-from-source-text. Figure-dependent items, 'select two answers' items, and free-response items on the page were skipped. A few numeric stems were lightly trimmed of their data tables where the answer follows from conservation alone; wording is otherwise verbatim.

questions.stem: A boy standing on a frictionless ice rink is initially at rest. He throws a snowball in the +x-direction, and it travels on a ballistic trajectory, hitting the ground some distance away. Which of the following is true about the boy while he is in the act of throwing the snowball?

choices.label: A
choices.text: He feels an upward force to compensate for the downward trajectory of the snowball.

choices.label: B
choices.text: He feels a backward force exerted by the snowball he is throwing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: He feels no net force.

choices.label: D
choices.text: He feels a forward force, the same force that propels the snowball.

questions.explanation: By Newton's third law, as the boy exerts a forward force on the snowball, the snowball exerts an equal and opposite (backward) force on the boy. That backward reaction force is what pushes the boy in the -x-direction (conservation of momentum on the frictionless rink).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim from OpenStax AP test-prep. OpenStax withholds the public answer key (instructor-only); answer independently verified from Newton's third law.

hints.prompt: Newton's third law states that when one object exerts a force on a second object, the second object exerts on the first a force that is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: equal in magnitude and in the same direction

choices.label: B
choices.text: equal in magnitude and in the opposite direction

choices.label: C
choices.text: larger in magnitude and in the opposite direction

choices.label: D
choices.text: present only when the first object is stationary

hints.rationale: Newton's third-law force pairs are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

hints.prompt: While the boy is throwing the snowball, which two forces form the relevant Newton's-third-law action–reaction pair?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the boy's push on the snowball and the snowball's push on the boy

choices.label: B
choices.text: the boy's push on the snowball and gravity acting on the snowball

choices.label: C
choices.text: the weight of the boy and the normal force from the ice

choices.label: D
choices.text: the snowball's weight and air resistance on the snowball

hints.rationale: The interaction is between the boy and the snowball, so the pair is the boy pushing the snowball and the snowball pushing back on the boy.

hints.prompt: Throwing the snowball horizontally changes the boy's motion. Which pair of the main options can be eliminated right away?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the 'upward force' option and the 'no net force' option

choices.label: B
choices.text: the 'backward force' option and the 'forward force' option

choices.label: C
choices.text: the 'no net force' option and the 'backward force' option

choices.label: D
choices.text: the 'upward force' option and the 'forward force' option

hints.rationale: A horizontal throw needs no vertical force, and the boy's motion changes so the net force is not zero—removing the 'upward' and 'no net force' options.

questions.stem: A student sets up an experiment with two air cars, A and B, of equal mass, moving on a linear, frictionless track. Before the collision, car A has a certain speed, and car B is at rest. Which of the following will be true about the total momentum of the two cars?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It will be greater before the collision.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It will be equal before and after the collision.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It will be greater after the collision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The answer depends on whether the collision is elastic or inelastic.

questions.explanation: On a frictionless track there is no net external horizontal force, so the system is isolated and total linear momentum is conserved. The total momentum is therefore the same before and after the collision, regardless of whether the collision is elastic or inelastic.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from conservation of momentum for an isolated system.

hints.prompt: For a system with no net external force, the total linear momentum:

choices.label: A
choices.text: increases over time

choices.label: B
choices.text: is conserved (stays constant)

choices.label: C
choices.text: decreases to zero

choices.label: D
choices.text: depends on whether internal collisions are elastic

hints.rationale: With no net external force the system is isolated, so total momentum is conserved.

hints.prompt: On the frictionless track, what is the net external horizontal force on the two-car system during the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: zero, because the track is frictionless and the forces between the cars are internal

choices.label: B
choices.text: a large forward force supplied by car A

choices.label: C
choices.text: a friction force that steadily removes momentum

choices.label: D
choices.text: it depends on whether the collision is elastic or inelastic

hints.rationale: Collision forces are internal and the frictionless track exerts no horizontal external force, so the net external force is zero.

hints.prompt: Momentum is a conserved quantity for an isolated system and cannot spontaneously grow or shrink. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'greater before the collision' and 'greater after the collision'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'equal before and after' and 'depends on elastic or inelastic'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'greater before the collision' and 'equal before and after'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'greater after the collision' and 'depends on elastic or inelastic'

hints.rationale: An isolated system's total momentum cannot be larger on one side, so 'greater before' and 'greater after' are both eliminated.

questions.stem: A test car is driving toward a solid crash-test barrier and is still moving when it hits the wall, after which it crumples and comes to a complete stop. In order to estimate the average force exerted by the wall on the car, what information would you need to collect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The (negative) acceleration of the car before it hits the wall and the distance the car travels while braking.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The (negative) acceleration of the car before it hits the wall and the velocity of the car just before impact.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The velocity of the car just before impact and the duration of the collision with the wall.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The duration of the collision with the wall and the distance the car travels while braking.

questions.explanation: The average force during impact follows from the impulse-momentum theorem: F_avg = Δp/Δt = m·v_impact / t_collision (final velocity is zero). With the car's mass known, you need the velocity just before impact and the duration of the collision. The pre-impact braking distance/acceleration are irrelevant to the wall's impact force.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the impulse-momentum theorem.

hints.prompt: The impulse–momentum theorem relates the average force during a collision to which quantities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: F_avg = Δp/Δt (change in momentum divided by collision time)

choices.label: B
choices.text: F_avg = Δp·Δt (momentum times time)

choices.label: C
choices.text: F_avg = ½mv² divided by distance

choices.label: D
choices.text: F_avg = mass times braking distance

hints.rationale: Impulse equals change in momentum, so F_avg = Δp/Δt.

hints.prompt: For the car that crumples to a stop against the wall, which quantities let you evaluate Δp/Δt (its mass is known)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the velocity just before impact and the duration of the collision

choices.label: B
choices.text: the braking acceleration before the wall and the braking distance

choices.label: C
choices.text: the make and color of the car

choices.label: D
choices.text: the distance the car travels while braking before impact

hints.rationale: Δp = m·v_impact (final speed is zero) and Δt is the collision time, so you need v_impact and the collision duration.

hints.prompt: The car's motion BEFORE it reaches the wall (its braking) does not set the wall's impact force. Which pair of options can be eliminated because they rely on that irrelevant braking information?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'acceleration before impact + braking distance' and 'collision duration + braking distance'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'acceleration before impact + velocity just before impact' and 'velocity just before impact + collision duration'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'velocity just before impact + collision duration' and 'collision duration + braking distance'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'acceleration before impact + braking distance' and 'velocity just before impact + collision duration'

hints.rationale: The pre-impact braking distance is irrelevant to the impact force, so both options that require it are eliminated.

questions.stem: Cart A is moving with an initial velocity +v (in the positive direction) toward cart B, initially at rest. Both carts have equal mass and are on a frictionless surface. Which of the following correctly characterizes the velocity of the center of mass of the system before and after the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: +v/2 before, -v/2 after

choices.label: B
choices.text: +v/2 before, 0 after

choices.label: C
choices.text: +v/2 before, +v/2 after

choices.label: D
choices.text: 0 before, 0 after

questions.explanation: The center-of-mass velocity is v_cm = (m·v + m·0)/(2m) = v/2. Because no net external force acts on the isolated system, the center-of-mass velocity is unchanged by the collision: it is +v/2 both before and after.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (center-of-mass velocity is conserved for an isolated system).

hints.prompt: For a system with no net external force, the velocity of the center of mass:

choices.label: A
choices.text: stays constant (is conserved)

choices.label: B
choices.text: always drops to zero after a collision

choices.label: C
choices.text: reverses direction during a collision

choices.label: D
choices.text: equals the velocity of the faster object

hints.rationale: With no external force, the center-of-mass velocity is unchanged.

hints.prompt: Which expression gives the center-of-mass velocity BEFORE the collision, for cart A (mass m, velocity +v) and cart B (mass m, at rest)?

choices.label: C
choices.text: m·v + m·0

hints.rationale: The center-of-mass velocity is total momentum divided by total mass: (m·v + m·0)/(2m).

hints.prompt: The center of mass is clearly moving in +x before the collision, and an isolated system's center-of-mass velocity does not reverse. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '0 before, 0 after' and '+v/2 before, −v/2 after'

choices.label: B
choices.text: '+v/2 before, +v/2 after' and '+v/2 before, 0 after'

choices.label: C
choices.text: '0 before, 0 after' and '+v/2 before, +v/2 after'

choices.label: D
choices.text: '+v/2 before, −v/2 after' and '+v/2 before, 0 after'

hints.rationale: v_cm before is +v/2 (not zero), and it cannot reverse to −v/2, so those two options are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two cars (A and B) of mass 1.5 kg collide. Car A is initially moving at 12 m/s, and car B is initially moving in the same direction with a speed of 6 m/s. The two cars are moving along a straight line before and after the collision. What will be the change in momentum of this system after the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: -27 kg·m/s

choices.label: B
choices.text: zero

choices.label: C
choices.text: +27 kg·m/s

choices.label: D
choices.text: It depends on whether the collision is elastic or inelastic.

questions.explanation: During a collision the only significant forces are the internal forces between the two cars, so the system is isolated and total momentum is conserved. The change in the system's total momentum is therefore zero, independent of whether the collision is elastic or inelastic.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from conservation of momentum.

hints.prompt: During a collision in which only internal forces act between the objects, the total momentum of the system:

choices.label: A
choices.text: changes by zero (is conserved)

choices.label: B
choices.text: increases

choices.label: C
choices.text: decreases

choices.label: D
choices.text: changes only if the collision is inelastic

hints.rationale: Internal collision forces cannot change the system's total momentum, so its change is zero.

hints.prompt: The masses (1.5 kg) and speeds (12 and 6 m/s) are given. To find the CHANGE in the system's total momentum, what actually determines the answer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: whether a net external force acts—if none, the change is zero regardless of the numbers

choices.label: B
choices.text: the product of the two masses

choices.label: C
choices.text: the difference of the two speeds times the mass

choices.label: D
choices.text: the kinetic energy before and after

hints.rationale: The change in total momentum depends only on external forces; with none, it is zero and the specific numbers are unneeded.

hints.prompt: The value ±27 kg·m/s equals the system's total momentum (1.5·12 + 1.5·6), not its change. Recognizing these as traps, which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '−27 kg·m/s' and '+27 kg·m/s'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'zero' and 'depends on elastic or inelastic'

choices.label: C
choices.text: '−27 kg·m/s' and 'zero'

choices.label: D
choices.text: '+27 kg·m/s' and 'depends on elastic or inelastic'

hints.rationale: ±27 kg·m/s is the total momentum itself (a distractor), not the change, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: Puck A (200 g) slides across a frictionless surface and collides with puck B (800 g), which is initially at rest. What is the change in momentum of the center of mass of the two-puck system as a result of the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: +1.6 kg·m/s

choices.label: B
choices.text: +0.8 kg·m/s

choices.label: D
choices.text: -1.6 kg·m/s

questions.explanation: The surface is frictionless and the collision forces are internal, so no net external force acts on the system. The total (center-of-mass) momentum is conserved, so its change as a result of the collision is zero.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim; the original stem referenced an experimental data table, trimmed here because the answer follows from conservation of momentum alone. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified.

hints.prompt: On a frictionless surface where only internal collision forces act, the total (center-of-mass) momentum of a system changes by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: zero

choices.label: B
choices.text: an amount equal to the heavier puck's momentum

choices.label: C
choices.text: an amount that depends on the mass ratio

choices.label: D
choices.text: twice the initial momentum

hints.rationale: With no external force, the system's total momentum is conserved, so its change is zero.

hints.prompt: For the two-puck system on the frictionless surface, what would be needed to change the system's total momentum during the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a net external force—but the puck-on-puck forces are internal, so there is none

choices.label: B
choices.text: friction from the surface

choices.label: C
choices.text: the normal force from the surface acting horizontally

choices.label: D
choices.text: gravity acting horizontally

hints.rationale: The surface is frictionless and the collision forces are internal, so there is no net external horizontal force.

hints.prompt: Since the total momentum of an isolated system cannot change, any nonzero value is a distractor. Which pair of nonzero options can be eliminated for the CHANGE in total momentum?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '+1.6 kg·m/s' and '−1.6 kg·m/s'

hints.rationale: A conserved total momentum has zero change, so the equal-and-opposite nonzero values are both eliminated.

questions.stem: Two cars (A and B) of equal mass have an elastic collision. Prior to the collision, car A is moving at 15 m/s in the +x-direction, and car B is moving at 10 m/s in the -x-direction. Assuming both cars continue moving along the x-axis after the collision, what will be the velocity of car A after the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the same as the original 15 m/s speed, opposite direction

choices.label: B
choices.text: equal to car B's velocity prior to the collision

choices.label: C
choices.text: equal to the average of the two velocities, in its original direction

choices.label: D
choices.text: equal to the average of the two velocities, in the opposite direction

questions.explanation: In a one-dimensional elastic collision between objects of equal mass, the two objects simply exchange velocities. Car A therefore leaves with car B's initial velocity (10 m/s in the -x-direction).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (equal-mass 1-D elastic collisions exchange velocities).

hints.prompt: In a one-dimensional ELASTIC collision between two objects of EQUAL mass, the objects:

choices.label: A
choices.text: exchange (swap) their velocities

choices.label: B
choices.text: both come to rest

choices.label: C
choices.text: stick together and move as one

choices.label: D
choices.text: keep their original velocities

hints.rationale: Equal-mass 1-D elastic collisions result in the two objects exchanging velocities.

hints.prompt: Which two facts stated in this problem tell you the velocity-exchange rule applies here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the collision is elastic and the two cars have equal mass

choices.label: B
choices.text: the collision is elastic and car B starts at rest

choices.label: C
choices.text: the cars have equal mass and momentum is not conserved

choices.label: D
choices.text: the cars move in opposite directions and stick together

hints.rationale: The velocity-exchange result requires a 1-D elastic collision between equal masses—both stated here.

hints.prompt: Averaging the two velocities describes a perfectly INELASTIC (stick-together) collision, not an elastic one. Which pair of options can be eliminated for this elastic collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'equal to the average of the two velocities, in its original direction' and 'equal to the average of the two velocities, in the opposite direction'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'the same 15 m/s speed, opposite direction' and 'equal to car B's velocity prior to the collision'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'equal to car B's velocity prior' and 'average of the two velocities, original direction'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the same 15 m/s speed, opposite direction' and 'average of the two velocities, opposite direction'

hints.rationale: The average-velocity outcomes correspond to a perfectly inelastic collision, so both 'average' options are eliminated here.

questions.stem: A rubber ball is dropped from rest at a fixed height. It bounces off a hard floor and rebounds upward, but it only reaches 90% of its original height. What is the best way to explain the loss of kinetic energy of the ball during the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Energy was required to deform the ball's shape during the collision with the floor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Energy was lost due to work done by the ball pushing on the floor during the collision.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Energy was lost due to friction between the ball and the floor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Energy was lost due to the work done by gravity during the motion.

questions.explanation: The bounce is inelastic: some kinetic energy is converted to internal energy (heat/sound) as the ball deforms and only partially restores its shape. This deformation accounts for the lost kinetic energy. Friction and gravity do not explain the energy 'lost' from the bounce itself.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the source text's treatment of inelastic collisions.

hints.prompt: When a bouncing ball loses kinetic energy in its collision with the floor, that energy is primarily:

choices.label: A
choices.text: converted into internal energy (heat/sound) as the ball deforms

choices.label: B
choices.text: destroyed and no longer exists

choices.label: C
choices.text: converted entirely into gravitational potential energy

choices.label: D
choices.text: transferred into the Earth's kinetic energy

hints.rationale: In an inelastic bounce, kinetic energy is converted to internal energy as the ball deforms.

hints.prompt: Reaching only 90% of the original height means mechanical energy was lost during the brief floor contact. In which phase is that energy lost?

choices.label: A
choices.text: during the deformation and partial restoration of the ball while it is in contact with the floor

choices.label: B
choices.text: during the free fall before impact

choices.label: C
choices.text: during the rise after the bounce

choices.label: D
choices.text: it is not actually lost

hints.rationale: The loss occurs during contact, when the ball deforms and only partly restores its shape.

hints.prompt: Gravity is a conservative force (it returns energy on the way up) and simple sliding friction is not what dissipates energy in a vertical bounce. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Energy was lost due to friction...' and 'Energy was lost due to the work done by gravity...'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Energy was required to deform the ball...' and 'Energy was lost due to friction...'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Energy was lost due to work done by the ball pushing on the floor...' and 'Energy was lost due to work done by gravity...'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Energy was required to deform the ball...' and 'Energy was lost due to work done by the ball pushing on the floor...'

hints.rationale: Gravity is conservative and friction is not the mechanism for a vertical bounce, so those two options are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two objects, A and B, have equal mass. Prior to the collision, mass A is moving 10 m/s in the +x-direction, and mass B is moving 4 m/s in the +x-direction. Which of the following results represents an inelastic collision between A and B?

choices.label: A
choices.text: After the collision, mass A is at rest, and mass B moves 14 m/s in the +x-direction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: After the collision, mass A moves 4 m/s in the -x-direction, and mass B moves 18 m/s in the +x-direction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: After the collision, the two masses stick together and move 7 m/s in the +x-direction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: After the collision, mass A moves 4 m/s in the +x-direction, and mass B moves 10 m/s in the +x-direction.

questions.explanation: All listed outcomes must conserve total momentum (10+4 = 14, in units of m·v). Option C (stick together at 7 m/s: 7+7 = 14) conserves momentum and loses kinetic energy (final ∝ 49+49 = 98 vs initial ∝ 100+16 = 116), which defines an inelastic collision. Options A and B would increase kinetic energy (impossible), and D conserves kinetic energy (elastic).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by checking momentum and kinetic-energy conservation for each option.

hints.prompt: An inelastic collision is one in which:

choices.label: A
choices.text: momentum is conserved but kinetic energy decreases

choices.label: B
choices.text: both momentum and kinetic energy increase

choices.label: C
choices.text: kinetic energy increases

choices.label: D
choices.text: momentum is not conserved

hints.rationale: Inelastic collisions conserve momentum while losing kinetic energy.

hints.prompt: The system's total momentum (per unit mass) is 10 + 4 = 14. Which condition must ANY valid outcome satisfy first, before you even check kinetic energy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the two final velocities must still sum to 14 (momentum conserved)

choices.label: B
choices.text: the two final velocities must sum to 28

choices.label: C
choices.text: kinetic energy must increase

choices.label: D
choices.text: the objects must move in opposite directions

hints.rationale: Any physical outcome must conserve momentum, so the final velocities must still total 14 (per unit mass).

hints.prompt: A collision can never INCREASE the total kinetic energy. Which pair of outcomes can be eliminated because they would require more kinetic energy after than before?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'A at rest, B moves 14 m/s +x' and 'A moves 4 m/s −x, B moves 18 m/s +x'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'the two stick together at 7 m/s +x' and 'A moves 4 m/s +x, B moves 10 m/s +x'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'A at rest, B moves 14 m/s +x' and 'the two stick together at 7 m/s +x'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'A moves 4 m/s −x, B moves 18 m/s +x' and 'A moves 4 m/s +x, B moves 10 m/s +x'

hints.rationale: Both of those outcomes have larger total kinetic energy than the initial value, which is impossible, so they are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two objects (A and B) of equal mass collide elastically. Mass A is initially moving 5.0 m/s in the +x-direction, and mass B is initially moving 3.0 m/s in the -x-direction. After the collision, mass A is moving with a velocity of 3.0 m/s in the -x-direction. What will be the velocity of mass B after the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 3.0 m/s in the +x-direction

choices.label: B
choices.text: 5.0 m/s in the +x-direction

choices.label: C
choices.text: 3.0 m/s in the -x-direction

choices.label: D
choices.text: 5.0 m/s in the -x-direction

questions.explanation: Equal-mass objects in a 1-D elastic collision exchange velocities. A starts at +5.0 and ends at -3.0 (B's initial velocity), and B starts at -3.0 and ends at +5.0 m/s. You can confirm with momentum conservation: 5.0 + (-3.0) = (-3.0) + v_B gives v_B = +5.0 m/s.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by velocity exchange and momentum conservation.

hints.prompt: For a 1-D elastic collision between EQUAL masses, a quick shortcut for the final velocities is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: swap the two initial velocities

choices.label: B
choices.text: set both final velocities to zero

choices.label: C
choices.text: add the two initial velocities together

choices.label: D
choices.text: reverse only the heavier object's velocity

hints.rationale: Equal-mass elastic collisions exchange the objects' velocities.

hints.prompt: Besides the velocity-exchange shortcut, which single principle lets you solve for B's final velocity from the other three known velocities?

choices.label: A
choices.text: conservation of total momentum

choices.label: B
choices.text: conservation of kinetic energy only

choices.label: C
choices.text: Newton's third law of motion

choices.label: D
choices.text: the impulse–momentum theorem applied to gravity

hints.rationale: With three of the four velocities known, conservation of momentum determines B's final velocity.

hints.prompt: The total momentum (per unit mass) is 5 + (−3) = +2, and A carries −3 after the collision, so B must move in +x. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '3.0 m/s in the −x-direction' and '5.0 m/s in the −x-direction'

choices.label: B
choices.text: '3.0 m/s in the +x-direction' and '5.0 m/s in the +x-direction'

choices.label: C
choices.text: '3.0 m/s in the +x-direction' and '3.0 m/s in the −x-direction'

choices.label: D
choices.text: '5.0 m/s in the +x-direction' and '5.0 m/s in the −x-direction'

hints.rationale: B must move in +x to keep the total momentum positive, so both −x options are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two objects of equal mass collide. Object A is initially moving in the +x-direction with a speed of 12 m/s, and object B is initially at rest. After the collision, object A is at rest, and object B is moving away with some unknown velocity. There are no external forces acting on the system of two masses. What statement can we make about this collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Momentum is conserved, but kinetic energy is not conserved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved.

choices.label: D
choices.text: More information is needed in order to determine which is conserved.

questions.explanation: With no external forces, momentum is conserved, so object B must move at 12 m/s (since A stops). Substituting back, the total kinetic energy before equals the total after (½m·12² in each case), so kinetic energy is also conserved — the collision is elastic.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from momentum and kinetic-energy conservation.

hints.prompt: If a collision leaves the total kinetic energy unchanged, the collision is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: elastic

choices.label: B
choices.text: perfectly inelastic

choices.label: C
choices.text: impossible

choices.label: D
choices.text: one in which momentum is not conserved

hints.rationale: A collision that conserves kinetic energy is by definition elastic.

hints.prompt: With no external forces and A ending at rest, momentum conservation (equal masses) forces B's final speed to be what, so you can then test kinetic energy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 12 m/s (equal to A's initial speed)

choices.label: B
choices.text: 6 m/s

choices.label: C
choices.text: 24 m/s

choices.label: D
choices.text: 0 m/s

hints.rationale: Momentum conservation (A stops, equal masses) gives B the full 12 m/s, which you then use to compare kinetic energies.

hints.prompt: The problem states there are no external forces, so momentum is definitely conserved and the situation is fully determined. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved.' and 'More information is needed...'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.' and 'Momentum is conserved, but kinetic energy is not...'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Neither momentum nor kinetic energy is conserved.' and 'Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Momentum is conserved, but kinetic energy is not...' and 'More information is needed...'

hints.rationale: No external forces guarantees momentum is conserved and the outcome is determinable, eliminating 'neither conserved' and 'more information needed'.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements is true about an inelastic collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Momentum is conserved, and kinetic energy is conserved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Momentum is conserved, and kinetic energy is not conserved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Momentum is not conserved, and kinetic energy is conserved.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Momentum is not conserved, and kinetic energy is not conserved.

questions.explanation: In any collision of an isolated system momentum is conserved. An inelastic collision is defined by a loss of kinetic energy (converted to heat, sound, or deformation). So momentum is conserved while kinetic energy is not.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from the definition of an inelastic collision.

hints.prompt: In ANY collision of an isolated system (no external forces), which quantity is always conserved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: total momentum

choices.label: B
choices.text: total kinetic energy

choices.label: C
choices.text: total mechanical potential energy

choices.label: D
choices.text: nothing is conserved

hints.rationale: Momentum is conserved in any isolated collision, elastic or inelastic.

hints.prompt: Two objects stick together in a collision and some kinetic energy is lost. What can you still say about the system's momentum (no external forces)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: it is conserved (unchanged)

choices.label: B
choices.text: it is lost along with the kinetic energy

choices.label: C
choices.text: it doubles

choices.label: D
choices.text: it cannot be determined

hints.rationale: Even when kinetic energy is lost, an isolated system's momentum is conserved.

hints.prompt: Momentum is conserved in every isolated collision. Which pair of options can be eliminated on that basis alone?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Momentum is not conserved, and kinetic energy is conserved.' and 'Momentum is not conserved, and kinetic energy is not conserved.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Momentum is conserved, and kinetic energy is conserved.' and 'Momentum is conserved, and kinetic energy is not conserved.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Momentum is conserved, and kinetic energy is conserved.' and 'Momentum is not conserved, and kinetic energy is conserved.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Momentum is not conserved, and kinetic energy is not conserved.' and 'Momentum is conserved, and kinetic energy is not conserved.'

hints.rationale: Both options claiming momentum is not conserved are eliminated, since isolated collisions always conserve momentum.

questions.stem: Mass A (2.0 kg) has an initial velocity of 4 m/s in the +x-direction. Mass B (2.0 kg) has an initial velocity of 5 m/s in the -x-direction. If the two masses have an elastic collision, what will be the final velocities of the masses after the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both will move 0.5 m/s in the -x-direction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Mass A will stop; mass B will move 9 m/s in the +x-direction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Mass B will stop; mass A will move 9 m/s in the -x-direction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mass A will move 5 m/s in the -x-direction; mass B will move 4 m/s in the +x-direction.

questions.explanation: The masses are equal, so the 1-D elastic collision exchanges their velocities. A (+4 m/s) takes B's velocity (5 m/s in -x), and B (-5 m/s) takes A's velocity (4 m/s in +x).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (equal-mass elastic collisions exchange velocities).

hints.prompt: Two equal masses collide elastically in one dimension. The result is that they:

choices.label: A
choices.text: exchange their velocities

choices.label: B
choices.text: both reverse and halve their speeds

choices.label: C
choices.text: both continue unchanged

choices.label: D
choices.text: come to rest

hints.rationale: Equal-mass 1-D elastic collisions swap the two velocities.

hints.prompt: Using the equal-mass elastic result, what will mass A's final velocity equal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: mass B's initial velocity

choices.label: B
choices.text: mass A's own initial velocity

choices.label: C
choices.text: the average of the two initial velocities

choices.label: D
choices.text: zero

hints.rationale: In an equal-mass elastic collision each object takes on the other's initial velocity.

hints.prompt: The total momentum (per unit mass) is 4 + (−5) = −1. Which pair of options can be eliminated because their final total momentum (±9) does not match?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Mass A will stop; mass B will move 9 m/s +x' and 'Mass B will stop; mass A will move 9 m/s −x'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Both will move 0.5 m/s −x' and 'A moves 5 m/s −x; B moves 4 m/s +x'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Both will move 0.5 m/s −x' and 'Mass A will stop; mass B will move 9 m/s +x'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Mass B will stop; mass A will move 9 m/s −x' and 'A moves 5 m/s −x; B moves 4 m/s +x'

hints.rationale: Those two give a total final momentum of ±9 (per unit mass), not −1, so they violate momentum conservation and are eliminated.

questions.stem: Mass A (2.0 kg) is moving with an initial velocity of 15 m/s in the +x-direction, and it collides with mass B (5.0 kg), initially at rest. After the collision, the two objects stick together and move as one. What is the change in kinetic energy of the system as a result of the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no change

choices.label: B
choices.text: decrease by 225 J

choices.label: C
choices.text: decrease by 161 J

choices.label: D
choices.text: decrease by 64 J

questions.explanation: Momentum conservation gives the common final velocity: v = (2.0·15)/(2.0+5.0) = 30/7 ≈ 4.29 m/s. Initial KE = ½·2.0·15² = 225 J; final KE = ½·7.0·(4.29)² ≈ 64 J. The change is about -161 J (a decrease of 161 J), typical of a perfectly inelastic collision.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

hints.prompt: In a perfectly inelastic collision, the change in the system's kinetic energy is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: negative (kinetic energy decreases)

choices.label: B
choices.text: zero

choices.label: C
choices.text: positive

choices.label: D
choices.text: always equal to the initial kinetic energy

hints.rationale: Perfectly inelastic collisions lose kinetic energy, so ΔKE is negative.

hints.prompt: To find ΔKE you first need the common final velocity. Which equation gives it?
hints.rationale: Momentum conservation for the stuck-together masses gives v = m_A v_A /(m_A + m_B).

hints.prompt: The masses keep moving after they stick, so kinetic energy drops but does NOT vanish (225 J is the entire initial KE). Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'no change' and 'decrease by 225 J'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'decrease by 161 J' and 'decrease by 64 J'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'no change' and 'decrease by 161 J'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'decrease by 225 J' and 'decrease by 64 J'

hints.rationale: KE must decrease (eliminating 'no change') but cannot drop by the full 225 J while the combined mass keeps moving, so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: Mass A slides across a rough table with an initial velocity of 12 m/s in the +x-direction. By the time mass A collides with mass B (a stationary object with equal mass), mass A has slowed to 10 m/s. After the collision, the two objects stick together, the velocity of the system is measured to be 5 m/s in the +x-direction, and the system eventually slides to a stop. Which of the following statements is true about this motion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Momentum is conserved during the collision, but it is not conserved during the motion before and after the collision.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Momentum is not conserved at any time during this analysis.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Momentum is conserved at all times during this analysis.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Momentum is not conserved during the collision, but it is conserved during the motion before and after the collision.

questions.explanation: During the brief collision the large internal contact forces dominate friction, so momentum is essentially conserved (10 m/s for one mass ≈ 5 m/s for the combined double mass). Before and after the collision, friction is an external force that steadily removes momentum (A slows 12→10, and the stuck pair slides to a stop), so momentum is not conserved during those phases.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (collision forces >> friction during impact; friction is an external force otherwise).

hints.prompt: The total momentum of a system is conserved only when:

choices.label: A
choices.text: there is no net external force on the system

choices.label: B
choices.text: the objects have equal mass

choices.label: C
choices.text: kinetic energy is also conserved

choices.label: D
choices.text: the objects are moving

hints.rationale: Conservation of momentum requires zero net external force.

hints.prompt: During the brief collision the internal contact forces are huge compared with friction, but before and after the collision friction acts alone. When is friction the important EXTERNAL force here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: before and after the collision (while sliding), not during the brief impact

choices.label: B
choices.text: only during the brief impact

choices.label: C
choices.text: never, because the table is frictionless

choices.label: D
choices.text: at all times equally

hints.rationale: Friction (an external force) dominates during the sliding phases but is negligible during the brief, high-force collision.

hints.prompt: The block visibly slows from friction (12→10, then to a stop), yet during the brief collision momentum is essentially conserved. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Momentum is not conserved at any time...' and 'Momentum is conserved at all times...'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'conserved during the collision, but not before and after' and 'not conserved during the collision, but conserved before and after'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Momentum is not conserved at any time...' and 'conserved during the collision, but not before and after'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'Momentum is conserved at all times...' and 'not conserved during the collision, but conserved before and after'

hints.rationale: Momentum is ~conserved during the impact (eliminating 'never') but friction removes it while sliding (eliminating 'always'), so both extremes are wrong.

questions.stem: Mass A is initially moving with a velocity of 24 m/s in the +x-direction. Mass B is twice as massive as mass A and is initially at rest. The two objects experience a totally inelastic collision. What is the final speed of both objects after the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A is not moving; B is moving 24 m/s in the +x-direction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Neither A nor B is moving.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A is moving 24 m/s in the -x-direction; B is not moving.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Both A and B are moving together 8 m/s in the +x-direction.

questions.explanation: In a totally inelastic collision the objects move together. Momentum conservation: m·24 = (m + 2m)·v, so v = 24/3 = 8 m/s in the +x-direction.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by calculation.

hints.prompt: In a totally inelastic collision, the objects afterward:

choices.label: A
choices.text: move together with a common velocity

choices.label: B
choices.text: move apart at equal speeds

choices.label: C
choices.text: both come to rest

choices.label: D
choices.text: exchange velocities

hints.rationale: Totally inelastic means the objects stick and move together.

hints.prompt: With A (mass m, 24 m/s) and B (mass 2m, at rest) sticking together, which momentum equation applies?

choices.label: D
choices.text: ½m·24² = ½(3m)·v²

hints.rationale: Momentum conservation: initial m·24 equals the combined mass (3m) times the common velocity.

hints.prompt: The total momentum before the collision points in +x and is not zero, so the stuck-together objects must move in +x. Which pair of options can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Neither A nor B is moving.' and 'A is moving 24 m/s −x; B is not moving.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'A is not moving; B is moving 24 m/s +x.' and 'Both A and B move together 8 m/s +x.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Neither A nor B is moving.' and 'A is not moving; B is moving 24 m/s +x.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'A is moving 24 m/s −x; B is not moving.' and 'Both A and B move together 8 m/s +x.'

hints.rationale: A zero or −x final motion violates conservation of the positive total momentum, so those two options are eliminated.

questions.stem: Mass A is initially moving with a velocity of 2 m/s in the +x-direction. Mass B is initially moving with a velocity of 6 m/s in the -x-direction. The two objects have equal masses. After they collide, mass A moves with a speed of 4 m/s in the -x-direction. What is the final velocity of mass B after the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 6 m/s in the +x-direction

choices.label: B
choices.text: 4 m/s in the +x-direction

choices.label: C
choices.text: zero

choices.label: D
choices.text: 4 m/s in the -x-direction

questions.explanation: Equal masses, so momentum conservation reads (per unit mass): 2 + (-6) = (-4) + v_B, i.e. -4 = -4 + v_B, giving v_B = 0. Mass B is at rest after the collision.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified by momentum conservation.

hints.prompt: For two objects of EQUAL mass, conservation of momentum can be written (per unit mass) as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: v(A,initial) + v(B,initial) = v(A,final) + v(B,final)

choices.label: B
choices.text: v(A,initial) − v(B,initial) = v(A,final) + v(B,final)

choices.label: C
choices.text: v(A,initial) × v(B,initial) = v(A,final) × v(B,final)

choices.label: D
choices.text: v(A,initial) + v(B,initial) = 0

hints.rationale: Equal masses cancel, so the sum of initial velocities equals the sum of final velocities.

hints.prompt: Taking +x as positive, what is the conserved total (per unit mass), i.e. the left side of 2 + (−6) = (−4) + v_B?
hints.rationale: 2 + (−6) = −4, the conserved total per unit mass.

hints.prompt: A student guesses that B simply reverses its own initial 6 m/s to become 6 m/s +x. Which listed option is this tempting but incorrect guess?

choices.label: A
choices.text: '6 m/s in the +x-direction'

choices.label: B
choices.text: '4 m/s in the +x-direction'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'zero'

choices.label: D
choices.text: '4 m/s in the −x-direction'

hints.rationale: Assuming B just reverses its 6 m/s gives '6 m/s +x', which ignores momentum conservation—a trap, not the answer.

questions.stem: Mass A (1.0 kg) slides across a frictionless surface with a velocity of 8 m/s in the positive direction. Mass B (3.0 kg) is initially at rest. The two objects collide and stick together. What will be the change in the center-of-mass velocity of the system as a result of the collision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: There will be no change in the center-of-mass velocity.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The center-of-mass velocity will decrease by 2 m/s.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The center-of-mass velocity will decrease by 6 m/s.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The center-of-mass velocity will decrease by 8 m/s.

questions.explanation: With no external force, the center-of-mass velocity cannot change. (Its value is v_cm = (1.0·8)/(1.0+3.0) = 2 m/s both before and after, which also happens to be the common final velocity once they stick.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified (center-of-mass velocity is conserved with no external force).

hints.prompt: On a frictionless surface, the center-of-mass velocity of a system during a collision:

choices.label: A
choices.text: does not change

choices.label: B
choices.text: drops to zero

choices.label: C
choices.text: doubles

choices.label: D
choices.text: reverses

hints.rationale: No external force means the center-of-mass velocity is unchanged.

hints.prompt: What is required to change a system's center-of-mass velocity, and is it present here (frictionless surface)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a net external force—absent here, so v_cm is unchanged

choices.label: B
choices.text: the internal collision forces—present, so v_cm changes

choices.label: C
choices.text: the masses being unequal

choices.label: D
choices.text: the objects sticking together

hints.rationale: Only a net external force changes v_cm; internal collision forces and sticking do not.

hints.prompt: The center of mass moves at v_cm = (1.0·8)/(1.0+3.0) = 2 m/s in +x. A decrease of 6 or 8 m/s would make it negative, which is impossible here. Which pair can be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'decrease by 6 m/s' and 'decrease by 8 m/s'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'no change' and 'decrease by 2 m/s'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'no change' and 'decrease by 6 m/s'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'decrease by 2 m/s' and 'decrease by 8 m/s'

hints.rationale: v_cm is only 2 m/s, so decreases of 6 or 8 m/s would drive it negative—impossible—so both are eliminated.

questions.stem: Two cars of equal mass approach an intersection. Car A is moving east at 45 m/s and car B is moving south at 35 m/s. They collide inelastically and stick together, moving as one object. Which of the following is true about the center-of-mass velocity of this system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The center-of-mass velocity will decrease after the collision as a result of lost energy (but not drop to zero).

choices.label: B
choices.text: The center-of-mass velocity will remain the same after the collision since momentum is conserved.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The center-of-mass velocity will drop to zero since the two objects stick together.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The magnitude of the center-of-mass velocity will remain the same, but the direction of the velocity will change.

questions.explanation: Center-of-mass velocity depends only on total momentum, which is conserved when no net external force acts. Even though kinetic energy is lost in the inelastic collision, the center-of-mass velocity (magnitude and direction) is unchanged.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax College Physics for AP® Courses 2e, Ch. 8 — Test Prep for AP® Courses
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax; live page also posts a no-LLM-training notice — SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Stem + 4 options scraped verbatim. OpenStax withholds the public answer key; answer independently verified from conservation of momentum (center-of-mass motion).

hints.prompt: The center-of-mass velocity of a system is determined by:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the total momentum divided by the total mass

choices.label: B
choices.text: the total kinetic energy

choices.label: C
choices.text: only the faster object's velocity

choices.label: D
choices.text: the amount of energy lost in the collision

hints.rationale: v_cm equals total momentum divided by total mass.

hints.prompt: This inelastic collision loses kinetic energy. Does losing kinetic energy change the total momentum (no external force)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: no—total momentum, and therefore v_cm, is unchanged

choices.label: B
choices.text: yes—momentum decreases along with the energy

choices.label: C
choices.text: yes—momentum drops to zero when they stick

choices.label: D
choices.text: only the direction of the momentum changes

hints.rationale: Energy loss does not affect momentum; with no external force, total momentum and v_cm are conserved.

hints.prompt: With momentum conserved, the center-of-mass velocity keeps the same magnitude AND direction. Which pair of options can be eliminated because they claim its magnitude changes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'will decrease after the collision (but not to zero)' and 'will drop to zero...'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'will remain the same...' and 'magnitude remains the same, but direction changes'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'will decrease after the collision (but not to zero)' and 'magnitude remains the same, but direction changes'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'will drop to zero...' and 'will remain the same...'

hints.rationale: Conserved momentum means v_cm's magnitude is unchanged, eliminating the 'decrease' and 'drop to zero' options.
